stories.headline: A Black Hole Just Woke Up After 100 Million Years — and It's Erupting Like a Cosmic Volcano
stories.hook: Scientists have captured the reawakening of a sleeping supermassive black hole that's now blasting jets nearly a million light-years long.
stories.body:
In the distant galaxy J1007+3540, a supermassive black hole sat dormant for roughly 100 million years. Then it woke up. New radio telescope images published this week show it shooting twin jets of plasma and energy stretching nearly a million light-years into surrounding space — a structure so vast it would span ten times the diameter of the Milky Way. Astronomers are calling it a "cosmic volcano" because it mirrors the behavior of geological volcanoes on Earth: dormant, then suddenly, violently active.

What makes this especially striking is that the black hole has erupted before. Researchers identified older, faded lobes of radio-emitting gas that mark a previous eruption from an earlier era — before the long silence. That means this system has a cyclical nature: active, dormant, active again. Scientists aren't sure what triggers the reawakening, but leading theories involve the black hole slowly gathering a fresh supply of gas and dust over millions of years until it finally has enough to ignite again.

The discovery is more than a spectacle. These so-called "restarted radio galaxies" are thought to play a major role in shaping the large-scale structure of the universe, injecting enormous amounts of energy into the surrounding gas and influencing whether or not new stars can form in neighboring regions. Mapping their cycles helps astronomers understand how galaxies — including our own — have evolved over cosmic time.

image.alt: A black hole surrounded by swirling gas and stars in deep space

links.title: Space.com: Reborn black hole erupting across 1 million light-years
Scientific American: Back from the dead — a black hole erupts after a 100-million-year hiatus
ScienceDaily: Study details

stories.headline: Webb Found Two Black Holes So Massive They Shouldn't Exist
stories.hook: These black holes make up 60% of their entire galaxy's mass — in a normal galaxy, that ratio is less than half a percent.
stories.body:
The James Webb Space Telescope has spotted something that shouldn't be possible: two dwarf galaxies whose central black holes are so enormous they account for roughly 60% of the galaxies' total stellar mass. In virtually every well-studied galaxy, the central black hole represents just 0.1 to 0.5% of the galaxy's mass. These objects are so far off the chart that astrophysicists have taken to calling them "overmassive" black holes.

The discovery adds to a growing pattern of anomalies Webb has been uncovering since it began observations. Earlier findings revealed mysterious compact objects nicknamed "little red dots" — small, red, seemingly very massive galaxies appearing far earlier in the universe's history than models predict. These new overmassive black holes may be related to that mystery, suggesting that in the early universe, black holes grew disproportionately fast relative to their host galaxies, flipping the normal co-evolutionary script.

The leading question now is how this happens. Did supermassive black holes form first, with galaxies building up around them? Or did some early galaxies have conditions that allowed black holes to devour material at extraordinary rates? Either way, the standard model of galaxy formation — in which stars and black holes grow together in a kind of regulated balance — is increasingly in need of revision. Webb, it seems, has an uncanny ability to find the exceptions that rewrite the rules.

image.alt: A stunning spiral galaxy with dense star clusters photographed by the Hubble Space Telescope
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: Phys.org: JWST spots overmassive black holes in dwarf galaxies
The Debrief: James Webb spots huge black holes where they shouldn't be

stories.headline: Every Single Apollo Moonwalker Got 'Lunar Hay Fever' — and Future Astronauts Have Reason to Worry
stories.hook: Not one of the twelve humans who walked on the Moon escaped without sneezing, red eyes, or an itchy throat.
stories.body:
A new report from the European Space Agency has confirmed what Apollo crews informally complained about for decades: lunar dust causes allergic-like symptoms in every person who comes into contact with it. All twelve astronauts who walked on the Moon reported sneezing fits, watery eyes, sore throats, and nasal congestion after returning to the lunar module. The dust clung to their suits, was tracked inside, and became airborne in the cabin's atmosphere. Some symptoms persisted for hours after exposure.

Lunar dust is unlike anything on Earth. Because the Moon has no atmosphere to cause weathering, individual dust particles remain jagged and razor-sharp at the microscopic scale — never smoothed by wind or water. It's also electrostatically charged, which makes it cling to everything and nearly impossible to brush off. When inhaled, this ultra-fine, jagged material is potentially damaging to lung tissue, and the ESA report raises concerns it could cause long-term pulmonary harm with sustained exposure.

This is no longer just an interesting historical footnote. NASA's Artemis III mission aims to land astronauts on the lunar surface for extended stays — potentially days rather than hours. Future lunar habitats, dust filtration systems, and spacesuit design will all need to account for the fact that Moon dust is essentially a respiratory hazard. Solving the dust problem may turn out to be one of the most unglamorous but critical engineering challenges of the next decade of human spaceflight.

image.alt: The lunar surface of the Moon's Taurus-Littrow Valley as photographed during the Apollo 17 mission
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: ESA: The toxic side of the Moon
AccuWeather: Lunar hay fever explained

stories.headline: A 250-Million-Year-Old Fossil Just Proved That Mammal Ancestors Laid Eggs
stories.hook: A fossil sitting in a museum since 2008 has finally cracked one of evolution's oldest mysteries — using a synchrotron the size of a stadium.
stories.body:
For years, paleontologists suspected that the ancestors of mammals laid eggs — but direct fossil evidence was maddeningly elusive. Now a team of researchers has confirmed it using a Lystrosaurus specimen found in South Africa in 2008. Using CT scanning and high-powered synchrotron X-ray imaging, they identified a fully formed embryo curled inside a fossilized egg dating back to the Early Triassic, roughly 250 million years ago. Lystrosaurus was a mammal-like reptile, part of the group called therapsids — the lineage that eventually gave rise to true mammals.

The significance can't be overstated. Lystrosaurus lived right at the boundary of the worst mass extinction in Earth's history — the Permian-Triassic extinction event, which wiped out more than 90% of marine species and 70% of land vertebrates. That this creature laid eggs and survived tells us something about how egg-laying reproductive strategies may have been an asset during such catastrophic times. The embryo inside the egg shows fully formed skeletal structures, suggesting the young were relatively developed at hatching — more like modern reptiles than the helpless young of today's mammals.

The discovery closes a long-standing gap in the fossil record. Scientists have long inferred that somewhere in mammalian prehistory, the transition from egg-laying to live birth occurred — but they lacked direct evidence of the egg-laying side. This find is the "smoking gun" that anchors that transition firmly in the Triassic world.

image.alt: A fossilized echinoid preserved in flint, showing detailed skeletal structure
image.credit: Photo by Birmingham Museums Trust on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: 250-million-year-old fossil proves mammal ancestors laid eggs
Scientific American: Ancient egg discovery
CNN: Mammal ancestors laid eggs — study confirms

stories.headline: The Lost World Under the North Sea Had Forests — With a Tree That Vanished 400,000 Years Ago
stories.hook: What is now open ocean between Britain and Europe was once covered in thick ancient forests — and new DNA evidence is revealing just how extraordinary they were.
stories.body:
Doggerland is the name archaeologists give to a vast stretch of land that once connected Britain to mainland Europe, now submerged beneath the North Sea. During the last Ice Age, it was home to ice-age hunters and megafauna. But a new study using ancient DNA extracted from 252 seabed sediment cores has revealed something even more remarkable: as recently as 16,000 years ago, Doggerland was blanketed in dense temperate forests of oak, elm, and hazel — thousands of years earlier than previously thought possible.

Most striking is the discovery of a walnut relative among the ancient pollen and plant DNA. This species had been considered extinct in northwest Europe for roughly 400,000 years — but it apparently clung to survival in the relatively mild refuge of Doggerland as ice sheets dominated the rest of the continent. When sea levels rose and the land drowned, it went extinct again.

The findings change our picture of what Ice Age Europe looked like and how plants and humans moved across the landscape. The forests of Doggerland would have been rich with wildlife and resources, and their loss — as rising seas flooded the land between about 9,000 and 6,000 years ago — represents one of the most dramatic environmental disruptions in human prehistory. For the people living there, the gradual loss of their homeland to the sea would have unfolded over generations, echoing in mythology and memory long after the land itself was gone.

image.alt: A tree submerged in clear blue-green water, its bare branches reaching upward
image.credit: Photo by Brad Pearson on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Doggerland forests discovered
University of Bradford: North Sea lost world findings
Ancient Origins: Doggerland ancient forest DNA

stories.headline: Neanderthals and Modern Humans Weren't Just Neighbors — They Were Collaborators
stories.hook: A cave in Israel suggests the two species were sharing tools, burial practices, and possibly beliefs about what happens after death.
stories.body:
For a long time, the narrative around Neanderthals and Homo sapiens was essentially one of displacement: modern humans arrived in Europe and the Middle East, and Neanderthals disappeared. But excavations at Tinshemet Cave in Israel are complicating that story significantly. New research published this month documents compelling evidence that the two species didn't just coexist — they actively shared culture, co-evolving practices that neither had fully developed alone.

The evidence includes tool assemblages from a transitional period roughly 110,000 years ago in which Neanderthal and modern human technologies appear side by side and intermingled. More striking is the presence of what appear to be shared burial customs — deliberate interments that mirror practices found in both lineages — suggesting that both species may have had shared concepts around death and possibly the afterlife. Researchers describe the Levant region as a "melting pot" during this period, where the two lineages met repeatedly and exchanged not just genes but ideas.

This reframes what we know about the origins of symbolic thought and cultural complexity. These behaviors — burying the dead, using symbolic objects — have traditionally been held up as markers of modern human cognition. If Neanderthals were developing them simultaneously, and possibly in conversation with early Homo sapiens, then the cognitive revolution that shaped our species may have been less a solo invention and more a collaborative emergence between closely related minds meeting on the borderlands of the ancient world.

image.alt: Ancient cave dwellings carved into a rocky hillside, with dark openings and layered stone
image.credit: Photo by Fatih Beki on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Neanderthals and Homo sapiens collaboration evidence

stories.headline: A Medieval Japanese Courtier Accidentally Helped Scientists Discover a Forgotten Solar Storm
stories.hook: A 13th-century court diary recorded mysterious red lights dancing in the northern sky — 800 years later, scientists figured out exactly what he was seeing.
stories.body:
Sometime around the year 1200, a Japanese court official dutifully noted in his diary a strange phenomenon: vivid red auroras visible in the night sky over Japan. At the time, it was a curiosity, perhaps a portent. Now, eight centuries later, that same record has become a crucial data point in one of the most remarkable feats of scientific detective work in recent memory.

Researchers combined that medieval diary entry with an extraordinary new technique: ultra-precise carbon-14 measurements taken from tree rings. When a major solar proton event strikes Earth, it leaves a distinctive spike in atmospheric carbon-14 that gets locked into tree rings for that specific year. By developing a new measurement method accurate to within a single year — a technique that took a decade to perfect — the team was able to match the courtier's description of aurora activity to a confirmed solar particle event around 1200 CE.

The implications go well beyond historical curiosity. The study also revealed that the solar cycle during the 13th century ran significantly shorter than today's — roughly 7 to 8 years rather than the modern 11 years. Understanding past solar activity patterns is increasingly important for predicting space weather events that can knock out satellites, disrupt GPS, and fry electrical grids. The more historical "anchors" scientists can identify in tree ring records, the better their models of the Sun's long-term behavior become — and the better prepared we can be for the next major solar storm.

image.alt: The aurora borealis reflected perfectly in the still surface of a dark lake at night
image.credit: Photo by Christoph Nolte on Unsplash

links.title: Smithsonian Magazine: Medieval writings and tree rings track a solar storm from 800 years ago
Gizmodo: Scientists used medieval poems and trees to uncover a 13th-century solar surge

stories.headline: Jellyfish Are Secretly Exploiting One of the Ocean's Weirdest Annual Events
stories.hook: Once a year, sea worms swarm to the surface and literally explode — and scientists have just caught jellyfish taking advantage of the carnage for the first time.
stories.body:
In the open ocean, certain species of polychaete worms engage in one of nature's most dramatic reproductive strategies: a brief, frenzied mass swarming event in which thousands of worms rise to the ocean surface and rupture, releasing eggs and sperm in an explosive cloud. The event is timed precisely to lunar cycles, lasts only a few hours, and has been known to science for some time. What wasn't known — until now — is that jellyfish are watching and waiting.

Marine biologists documented for the first time jellyfish actively converging on swarming polychaete worms and feeding heavily during these brief eruption events. The jellyfish appeared to be responding to cues — likely chemical signals in the water — that allowed them to anticipate or detect the swarm, positioning themselves to feast on a sudden abundance of food that otherwise doesn't exist. The behavior had simply never been caught on camera or described in the scientific literature before.

The finding suggests that jellyfish are far more behaviorally sophisticated and ecologically opportunistic than their reputation as simple drifters implies. It also raises questions about how many other predators silently exploit these brief biological bonanzas — and whether the timing of polychaete swarms, which is sensitive to water temperature and lunar phase, could shift under climate change in ways that disrupt the creatures that have learned to depend on them.

image.alt: A translucent jellyfish glowing softly as it drifts through blue ocean water
image.credit: Photo by Michal Mrozek on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: Jellyfish feeding behavior discovery

stories.headline: Living With Roommates Is Quietly Reshaping Your Gut Microbiome
stories.hook: The bacteria living inside you are influenced by the people who share your roof — even when you don't share meals.
stories.body:
Your gut microbiome — the trillions of bacteria that live in your digestive system and affect everything from immune function to mood — is partly shaped by the people you live with. That's the takeaway from a new study that tracked the gut bacteria of Seychelles warblers living in groups, finding that cohabitants share significantly more microbial strains than birds living apart. When researchers cross-referenced the findings with human data, the same pattern emerged: housemates have more similar gut microbiomes than strangers, even after controlling for diet.

The mechanism appears to involve direct physical contact and shared environments rather than shared food. Certain anaerobic bacterial strains — types that struggle to survive outside a host — require close personal contact to transfer between individuals. Simply living in the same space, touching the same surfaces, and breathing the same air is enough to gradually share these microbes over time. The effect strengthens the longer two individuals live together.

The implications are wide-ranging. It suggests that the social structure of who we live with may have measurable effects on health outcomes mediated through microbiome changes — and raises intriguing questions about what happens to your microbiome when you move out, get divorced, or move into a care home. Conversely, it raises the possibility that cohabitation with healthy individuals could itself be a kind of probiotic — and that social isolation might have microbial consequences we haven't fully appreciated.

image.alt: Purple-stained Clostridioides difficile bacteria under a microscope, from a CDC illustration

links.title: ScienceDaily: Cohabitation and gut microbiome sharing
Interesting Engineering: Your roommate can change your gut

stories.headline: Scientists Just Solved a 50-Year Ocean Mystery — and the Answer Is Bad News for Climate
stories.hook: Oceans have been silently releasing methane in a way that defied explanation for half a century. Now we know why — and it could get worse.
stories.body:
For decades, oceanographers were puzzled by a paradox: surface seawater, despite being well-oxygenated, kept releasing methane — a potent greenhouse gas that shouldn't be produced in oxygen-rich environments. This was called the "oceanic methane paradox," and it has resisted explanation since the 1970s. A new study has finally cracked it. The culprit is a group of microbes that, when starved of phosphorus and nitrogen, switch to an alternative metabolic pathway that produces methane as a byproduct — even in the presence of oxygen.

The mechanism works like this: in the open ocean, nutrients like phosphorus can be scarce. When certain marine microbes run low on these essential nutrients, they begin breaking down organic phosphorus compounds called phosphonates to scavenge what they need. That breakdown process generates methane as a side product. The microbes weren't "trying" to produce methane — it was simply an unavoidable chemical consequence of their survival strategy.

The troubling part is what happens next. Climate change is expected to increase the stratification of the ocean — essentially layering warm, nutrient-poor water on top, while deeper nutrient-rich water stays trapped below. That nutrient depletion at the surface would cause more microbes to fall back on phosphonate breakdown, producing more methane, which warms the atmosphere more, which stratifies the ocean further. It's a feedback loop, and scientists are now working urgently to quantify just how significant a role this cycle could play in climate projections that have, until now, largely left it out of the models.

image.alt: Powerful waves crashing against a rocky coastline at sunset with orange-tinted sky
image.credit: Photo by engin akyurt on Unsplash

links.title: Phys.org: Hidden ocean feedback loop and climate change
ScienceDaily: Oceanic methane paradox solved

stories.headline: A Common Industrial Chemical May Be Behind a Hidden Parkinson's Epidemic
stories.hook: A solvent used in dry cleaning, metal degreasing, and decaf coffee has soaked into groundwater across the country — and one study found it raises Parkinson's risk by 500%.
stories.body:
Trichloroethylene — known as TCE — was once a miracle solvent. For most of the 20th century it was used to degrease metal parts in factories, clean clothes at dry cleaners, decaffeinate coffee, and even as a surgical anesthetic. It was cheap, effective, and seemed relatively harmless. We now know otherwise. TCE evaporates readily into air and leaches into groundwater, and it has been detected at thousands of contaminated sites across the United States — including under residential neighborhoods, schools, and military bases like Camp Lejeune.

A new wave of epidemiological research is linking TCE exposure to dramatically elevated Parkinson's disease risk. The chemical crosses the blood-brain barrier and is selectively toxic to dopaminergic neurons — the exact cells destroyed in Parkinson's. A small occupational study found workers with high TCE exposure had roughly a 500% greater risk of developing the disease. A larger study of Camp Lejeune veterans — who drank TCE-contaminated water for years — found a 70% elevated risk even at lower exposure levels.

Parkinson's rates in the United States have nearly doubled over the past 25 years, a trend that can't be explained by aging demographics alone. Environmental neurotoxins like TCE are increasingly suspected as a major driver — not a sole cause, but a critical contributor interacting with genetic predisposition. The frustrating reality is that TCE was banned in many consumer products decades ago but remains legal for industrial use and continues to contaminate groundwater from legacy sites. Cleaning up those sites has been slow, underfunded, and politically contested.

image.alt: An anatomical model of a human head showing the brain structure in detail
image.credit: Photo by Marek Piwnicki on Unsplash

links.title: AAAS Science: Widely-used chemical strongly linked to Parkinson's disease
UCSF: Chemical exposure may raise Parkinson's risk

stories.headline: The Largest-Ever Study on Medical Cannabis Just Delivered an Uncomfortable Finding
stories.hook: The conditions medicinal cannabis is most commonly prescribed for may be the ones it least effectively treats.
stories.body:
Medicinal cannabis has been increasingly prescribed — and marketed — as a treatment for anxiety, depression, and PTSD. The pitch is intuitive: cannabis is relaxing, stress-reducing, and many patients report feeling better after using it. But the largest systematic review of the evidence ever conducted has arrived at a strikingly different conclusion. Across dozens of randomized controlled trials and observational studies, researchers found no meaningful improvement in anxiety symptoms, depression scores, or PTSD outcomes compared to placebo in patients using medicinal cannabis.

This isn't a fringe finding or a single poorly-designed study. The review pooled data from thousands of patients across multiple countries and multiple formulations of cannabis and cannabidiol. The effect sizes for mental health outcomes were consistently small and failed to reach clinical significance — meaning that even when there was a measurable difference, it wasn't large enough to matter in a patient's daily life. The authors are careful to note that cannabis may still be beneficial for other conditions it's prescribed for, including chronic pain and certain types of epilepsy.

The disconnect between patient experience and clinical trial data is a puzzle researchers are actively trying to understand. One hypothesis is that the subjective feeling of relief cannabis provides — real and genuinely felt — doesn't translate into lasting changes in anxiety or mood architecture. Another is that the placebo effect is exceptionally powerful for conditions like anxiety. Whatever the reason, the study is a significant counterweight to the multi-billion-dollar medicinal cannabis industry's marketing claims.

image.alt: Close-up of cannabis leaves with visible texture and detail
image.credit: Photo by Jeff W on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: Largest-ever medicinal cannabis mental health study

stories.headline: A Cheap Blood Test Can Now Detect Multiple Cancers Before You Feel a Single Symptom
stories.hook: Mayo Clinic researchers have developed a screening tool using DNA methylation patterns that could catch cancer — and other serious diseases — years before diagnosis.
stories.body:
Early cancer detection has long been the holy grail of oncology. Catch a tumor before it spreads, and survival rates skyrocket. The problem has always been cost, accessibility, and the lack of a single test that can screen for multiple cancer types at once. A new test called MethylScan, developed by researchers at the Mayo Clinic, may be a significant step toward solving all three problems simultaneously.

MethylScan works by analyzing patterns of DNA methylation — chemical tags on DNA that regulate gene expression — in a standard blood sample. Cancer cells shed DNA into the bloodstream, and their methylation patterns are characteristically different from healthy cells. The test can detect these abnormal signatures and not only flag the presence of cancer but help identify which organ or tissue it likely originated in. In early trials, it demonstrated the ability to detect multiple cancer types at early stages, as well as other serious conditions like organ damage, before conventional symptoms appear.

What makes MethylScan especially promising is its potential price point. Unlike some liquid biopsy tests that require complex, expensive genomic sequencing, methylation analysis can be done more affordably — putting it within reach of broader population screening programs rather than just wealthy patients or those with known high risk. The researchers envision it as an annual blood draw at a regular checkup, the cancer equivalent of a cholesterol test. It's still in clinical validation, but if the results hold up, it represents a genuine shift in how medicine might detect and prevent some of its most serious diseases.

image.alt: Glass vials of blood samples arranged in a clinical laboratory setting at the NIH Clinical Center
image.credit: Photo by National Cancer Institute on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: MethylScan blood test can detect cancer early

stories.headline: A New AI Design Uses 100 Times Less Energy — and Actually Works Better
stories.hook: By borrowing a page from how humans actually reason, researchers at Tufts built an AI system that uses just 1% of the training power while outperforming standard models by a factor of three.
stories.body:
Artificial intelligence's dirty secret is its electricity bill. Training large language models consumes staggering amounts of power — AI is already estimated to consume more than 10% of US electricity, a figure that's rising rapidly. Researchers at Tufts University may have found a fundamentally different path forward. Their approach, called neuro-symbolic AI, combines traditional neural networks with symbolic reasoning — essentially teaching a model to break problems into logical steps rather than trying to brute-force solutions through pattern matching alone.

The results are striking. On a set of complex reasoning tasks, the neuro-symbolic model achieved a 95% success rate compared to 34% for standard neural network approaches — nearly three times better. More remarkably, it accomplished this using roughly 1% of the training energy that conventional models require, a reduction of about 100-fold. The secret is that symbolic reasoning allows the model to generalize from far fewer examples, rather than needing to see millions of training cases to learn a concept.

The implications for AI's sustainability — and for the economics of deploying AI at scale — are significant. The current trajectory has AI energy consumption on course to rival that of entire countries within a decade. A 100-fold efficiency gain doesn't solve the problem overnight, but it changes the math considerably. It also points to a broader lesson: the most powerful AI systems of the future may not be the ones with the most raw computing power, but the ones that reason most cleverly — much like the human minds they're ultimately trying to emulate.

image.alt: An artistic illustration of artificial intelligence and neural networks rendered in glowing geometric shapes
image.credit: Photo by Google DeepMind on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: New AI models could slash energy use while dramatically improving performance
Tufts Now: New AI models could slash energy use
SciTechDaily: 100x less power — the breakthrough that could solve AI's energy crisis

stories.headline: Quantum Encryption Just Crossed a Distance Barrier That Once Seemed Impossible
stories.hook: Researchers have sent truly unhackable encryption keys across 120 kilometers of fiber — a distance that puts real-world quantum networks within practical reach.
stories.body:
Quantum key distribution — the use of quantum mechanics to generate encryption keys that are physically impossible to intercept without detection — has long been seen as the ultimate in communication security. The problem has always been distance: quantum states are fragile, and maintaining them across long fiber-optic links has been extraordinarily difficult. A new breakthrough using quantum dot time-bin encoding has pushed stable key distribution to 120 kilometers, a record that meaningfully closes the gap between lab demonstrations and real-world telecommunications infrastructure.

The technique uses precisely engineered quantum dots — tiny semiconductor structures that emit single photons on demand — to encode information in the timing of light pulses rather than in their polarization or other properties that degrade more quickly over distance. This makes the signal significantly more robust over long fiber spans. The system achieved stable key generation rates suitable for practical encryption at the full 120 km distance, with error rates low enough to maintain security guarantees.

The significance is practical as well as scientific. Current internet encryption relies on mathematical problems that are hard for classical computers to solve — but will likely be crackable by quantum computers once they mature. Quantum key distribution offers a fundamentally different approach whose security doesn't depend on computational hardness but on the laws of physics themselves. Getting that technology to work over real-world distances, in standard fiber-optic infrastructure, is the necessary step before it can protect real communications. At 120 km, city-to-city quantum-encrypted links are starting to look genuinely achievable.

image.alt: A bundle of fiber optic cables glowing with white and blue light in a dark room
image.credit: Photo by Denny Müller on Unsplash

links.title: SciTechDaily: Quantum encryption keys sent over 120km using quantum dots
